name: redb
description: redb is a simple, portable, high-performance, ACID-compliant embedded key-value store written in pure Rust. It uses a collection of copy-on-write B-trees for storage, providing MVCC support that allows a writer and concurrent readers to operate without blocking each other. The entire database is stored in a single file, making it easy to manage and deploy. redb is thread-safe by default and exposes a type-safe API via TableDefinition and MultimapTableDefinition, supporting arbitrary Rust types as keys and values. It is loosely inspired by LMDB but implemented entirely in Rust with no C dependencies, making it easy to cross-compile and embed in any Rust project.
core_strengths: Pure Rust implementation with zero C dependencies, easy to cross-compile
MVCC with concurrent readers and writer via copy-on-write B-trees
Fully ACID-compliant transactions with crash safety by default
Type-safe typed table API supporting arbitrary Rust key and value types
Single-file storage format, simple to deploy and back up
Savepoints and rollbacks for fine-grained transaction control
best_use_cases: Rust desktop applications needing an embedded durable store
Configuration and metadata storage in Rust CLI tools and daemons
Local caching layers with ACID guarantees in Rust services
Edge and IoT applications where pure Rust cross-compilation matters
Replacements for LMDB in pure-Rust codebases
remarks: redb uses a single-writer model: only one write transaction at a time. Concurrent readers are non-blocking thanks to MVCC, but a long-running read transaction will prevent the database file from shrinking during compaction. The database file size grows monotonically and requires manual compaction (via Database::compact()) to reclaim space — there is no automatic background compaction. Keys in standard tables are stored in lexicographic byte order. The maximum key size is limited by the B-tree page size (default 4 KB). redb is embedded-only with no built-in network server or replication protocol.
